attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents with a two-week history of upper abdominal pain and fever. The pain occasionally radiates to the shoulder. He recently returned from a one-month trip to Thailand, during which he experienced a bout of bloody diarrhoea but made a full recovery. However, upon returning to Australia, he began to feel increasingly lethargic. The patient is normally in good health and does not have any pre-existing conditions.

On examination his blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg, pulse 90/min and temperature 38.5°C. His abdomen is soft apart from tenderness in the right upper quadrant. Blood tests reveal leukocytosis and elevated ALP levels, while bilirubin levels remain within the normal range. A CT scan is performed and a representative slice shown (image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely causative organism?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is suffering from amoebic liver abscess, which is the most common extraintestinal manifestation of Entamoeba histolytica infection. This protozoan parasite is transmitted through the faecal-oral route and is most commonly seen in India, Africa, South Asia and South America. His travel history to Thailand and a prior episode of dysentery raise the possibility of amoebic liver abscess as the diagnosis.

This CT scan shows multiple intrahepatic lesions that are well-defined, hypodense masses with peripheral enhancing rims. The majority of amoebic liver abscesses are solitary lesions. Diagnosis is made using a serology test for antibodies to E. histolytica along with imaging findings. Aspiration of abscesses is not necessary for establishing the diagnosis, but if aspirated, they contain acellular debris that forms a brown, thick fluid (classically known as "anchovy paste").

Pyogenic liver abscess presents similarly to amoebic liver abscess and distinguishing between these two diseases based on clinical courses and imaging findings can be very challenging. With regards to pyogenic abscess formation:

  • Underlying biliary tract disease, such as gallstones or malignant obstruction, is present in about half of the cases. A considerable proportion occurs after seeding through the porta hepatis, such as post-bowel leakage and peritonitis.

  • Most cases are polymicrobial, but enteric gram-negative bacilli, particularly Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae, are the most commonly identified pathogens.

  • K. pneumoniae is an important cause of community-acquired primary invasive liver abscess syndrome in East Asia (especially Taiwan), and diabetes mellitus appears to be a prominent risk factor.

Pseudomonas aeruginosa can cause a variety of infections in both immunocompetent and immunocompromised hosts, and antimicrobial resistance is increasing. However, it is not a common cause of liver abscess.

Proteus mirabilis is notorious for causing urinary tract infections, including cystitis and pyelonephritis, but it is not associated with the development of liver abscess.
learningPoints: Entamoeba histolytica infection can manifest as liver abscess, which is characterised by RUQ pain, fever and hepatomegaly.
references: Anesi JA, Gluckman S. Amebic liver abscess. Clin Liver Dis (Hoboken) [Internet]. 2015 Aug [cited 2023 Jul 10];6(2):41. Available from: https://aasldpubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cld.488 doi: 10.1002/cld.488

Longworth S, Han J. Pyogenic liver abscess. Clin Liver Dis (Hoboken) [Internet]. 2015 Aug [cited 2023 Jul 10];6(2):51. Available from: https://aasldpubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cld.487 doi: 10.1002/cld.487
